prompt: In the field of FinTech, machine learning algorithms are now widely applied to asset allocation and investment decisions. Examples include classic models like Mean-Variance and Black-Litterman, as well as emerging deep learning models. While these models have shown certain advantages under different market conditions, each also has its limitations. For instance, the Mean-Variance model assumes asset returns follow a normal distribution, which often doesn't align with actual market conditions. The Black-Litterman model relies on subjective view inputs, introducing a degree of subjectivity. Although deep learning models can handle complex non-linear relationships, they suffer from poor interpretability. So, what are the core differences between these various models in terms of risk measurement, return prediction, and asset allocation? And is it possible to combine their strengths to build a more general-purpose and effective modeling framework?
article:
# Comparing Mean-Variance, Black-Litterman, and Deep Learning for Asset Allocation: Toward a Hybrid FinTech Portfolio Framework

## Executive Summary

**Direct answer to the first question.** In contemporary FinTech asset allocation, **Mean-Variance Optimization (MVO)**, **Black-Litterman (BL)**, and **deep learning (DL)** differ most in **where they place modeling effort** in the investment stack.

- **MVO** is mainly an **allocation engine**: it takes expected returns and a covariance matrix as inputs and converts them into portfolio weights. Its advantage is transparency and constraint-handling; its core weakness is that it can **magnify small estimation errors** in expected returns or covariances into extreme weights and high turnover [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[1]](https://www.andrew.cmu.edu/user/gc0v/webpub/estimationerror.pdf).
- **BL** is mainly a **Bayesian return-regularization layer** on top of mean-variance logic: it starts from market-implied equilibrium returns and blends them with views and confidence. Its advantage is more stable and diversified allocations than naïve MVO; its cost is that it makes **subjectivity explicit** through view selection, confidence calibration, and parameters such as \( \tau \) and \( \Omega \) [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf)[[58]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1800205/FULLTEXT01.pdf).
- **DL** is mainly a **prediction and representation-learning family**: it can learn nonlinear return signals, regimes, or direct policies from structured and alternative data. Its advantage is modeling flexibility; its main weaknesses are **interpretability, instability under nonstationarity, validation difficulty, and governance burden**, especially when used end-to-end [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf)[[146]](https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P14112024.pdf).

**Direct answer to the second question.** A **hybrid framework is feasible and, in practice, more defensible than any standalone family as a general-purpose design**. The strongest contemporary pattern is modular:
1. **DL** for signal extraction, regime detection, or uncertainty estimation;
2. **BL-style Bayesian updating** to regularize those signals into posterior expected returns with confidence-aware shrinkage;
3. **constrained classical optimization** to enforce diversification, risk budgets, turnover limits, transaction-cost control, and governance discipline [[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf)[[53]](https://www.engineeringletters.com/issues_v29/issue_2/EL_29_2_40.pdf)[[121]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.11273)[[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1)[[123]](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.01918v3).

That hybrid is not a universal winner. It is viable only if it is backed by **walk-forward validation, explicit cost modeling, uncertainty calibration, drift monitoring, and human oversight** [[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf)[[87]](https://www.imf.org/-/media/files/publications/gfsr/2024/october/english/ch3.pdf)[[89]](https://www.dechert.com/knowledge/onpoint/2023/9/sec-proposes-new-regulatory-framework-for-use-of-ai-by-broker-de.html)[[146]](https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P14112024.pdf)[[156]](https://www.iif.com/portals/0/Files/content/Innovation/2024%20IIF-EY%20Survey%20Report%20on%20AI_ML%20Use%20in%20Financial%20Services_Public%2001.08.25.pdf).

**Current-state context.** The market and regulatory evidence supports that conclusion. ESMA reports that funds formally promoting AI remain a **minor share of the industry**, peaked in **2023**, and have shown **no clear industry-wide performance dominance**, with mixed investor flows [[86]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2025-02/ESMA50-43599798-9923_TRV_Article_Artificial_intelligence_in_EU_investment_funds.pdf). In ESMA’s **2026** survey covering **728 entities across 19 EU countries**, adoption remained **partial and uneven**: **45%** of respondents reported **no AI use cases** in production or development, **65%** of micro firms had no active or planned AI use cases, while **96%** of large firms were already using AI or planning to do so; at the same time, **70%** expected AI-related investment to increase between **2025 and 2027** [[88]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2026-02/ESMA50-481369926-30599_TRV_Risk_Analysis_AI_adoption_and_trends_in_securities_markets.pdf). The implication is not “deep learning has replaced portfolio theory,” but rather that AI is being inserted into selected layers while classical/Bayesian methods remain central in the decision and control layer [[86]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2025-02/ESMA50-43599798-9923_TRV_Article_Artificial_intelligence_in_EU_investment_funds.pdf)[[87]](https://www.imf.org/-/media/files/publications/gfsr/2024/october/english/ch3.pdf)[[88]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2026-02/ESMA50-481369926-30599_TRV_Risk_Analysis_AI_adoption_and_trends_in_securities_markets.pdf).

**Reasonable assumptions used in this review.**
1. **Liquid public-market portfolios** in asset-management/FinTech settings;  
2. **Multi-asset or multi-security allocation**, not single-name discretionary stock picking;  
3. **Daily to monthly rebalancing horizons**;  
4. **Long-only or moderately constrained portfolios** unless otherwise noted;  
5. **Realistic implementation issues** matter: transaction costs, turnover, model risk, governance, and out-of-sample stability.

---

## Assumptions, Scope, and Evaluation Criteria

This report is limited to **FinTech asset allocation and investment decision-making**. It excludes unrelated FinTech domains such as payments or fraud detection except where regulatory AI governance affects investment-model deployment [[87]](https://www.imf.org/-/media/files/publications/gfsr/2024/october/english/ch3.pdf)[[86]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2025-02/ESMA50-43599798-9923_TRV_Article_Artificial_intelligence_in_EU_investment_funds.pdf).

The comparison is structured around a practical decomposition that is often blurred in marketing and even in academic comparisons:

- **Return model**: how expected returns, views, or signals are formed;  
- **Risk model**: how uncertainty, covariance, downside risk, or tail risk are measured;  
- **Allocation engine**: how those inputs become implementable portfolio weights [[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf).

Using that decomposition avoids a category error. **BL is not primarily a replacement for portfolio optimization**; it is a different way of forming expected returns. **DL is not one model**; it can be a forecasting model, a policy learner, or an end-to-end portfolio network. **MVO is not a return predictor**; it is an optimizer that depends heavily on upstream estimates [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf)[[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591).

Seven evaluation dimensions are used consistently throughout:

1. **Risk measurement** — variance/covariance, posterior uncertainty, tail risk, regime sensitivity, stress testing, robustness [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf)[[132]](https://arxiv.org/html/2604.14206v1)  
2. **Return prediction** — historical estimates, equilibrium priors, subjective views, factor signals, supervised forecasts, sequence models, alternative data [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)  
3. **Portfolio construction** — objective, constraints, turnover, transaction costs, rebalancing [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf)[[121]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.11273)  
4. **Interpretability and governance** — auditability, explainability, compliance suitability [[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf)[[150]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2022/october/artificial-intelligence)[[156]](https://www.iif.com/portals/0/Files/content/Innovation/2024%20IIF-EY%20Survey%20Report%20on%20AI_ML%20Use%20in%20Financial%20Services_Public%2001.08.25.pdf)  
5. **Robustness** — estimation-error sensitivity, overfitting, structural breaks, model drift [[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)  
6. **Data requirements** — data volume, modality, and infrastructure burden [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[88]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2026-02/ESMA50-481369926-30599_TRV_Risk_Analysis_AI_adoption_and_trends_in_securities_markets.pdf)  
7. **Regime suitability** — bull, bear, crisis, high-volatility, low-signal, and structural-break environments [[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf)[[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf)[[86]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2025-02/ESMA50-43599798-9923_TRV_Article_Artificial_intelligence_in_EU_investment_funds.pdf)

---

## Introduction: Asset Allocation in Contemporary FinTech

The central practical fact in 2024–2026 FinTech asset allocation is **not** that deep learning has displaced classical portfolio construction. It is that firms are increasingly **decomposing the investment process into modules**, using AI where it adds predictive or analytical value while retaining interpretable optimization and human oversight in portfolio assembly [[87]](https://www.imf.org/-/media/files/publications/gfsr/2024/october/english/ch3.pdf)[[86]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2025-02/ESMA50-43599798-9923_TRV_Article_Artificial_intelligence_in_EU_investment_funds.pdf)[[88]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2026-02/ESMA50-481369926-30599_TRV_Risk_Analysis_AI_adoption_and_trends_in_securities_markets.pdf).

Two pieces of evidence matter here.

First, the **research pipeline has expanded sharply**. One systematic review of deep learning in financial asset management identified **934** Scopus articles, of which **612** met inclusion criteria, covering applications such as forecasting, portfolio management, factor investing, and trading [[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591). Another review focused on financial forecasting found **187** Scopus-indexed studies published from **2020 to 2024**, with **RNN/LSTM** architectures still dominant and **CNN-LSTM hybrids** growing as researchers try to capture joint spatial-temporal structure [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822). This shows that DL is no longer peripheral in investment research.

Second, the **adoption curve in industry remains uneven and governance-constrained**. ESMA’s findings that AI-promoting funds are still a minor share of the industry, and that many firms—especially smaller ones—still have no production or planned AI use cases, imply that practical deployment depends on data scale, infrastructure, and compliance capacity, not just algorithmic promise [[86]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2025-02/ESMA50-43599798-9923_TRV_Article_Artificial_intelligence_in_EU_investment_funds.pdf)[[88]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2026-02/ESMA50-481369926-30599_TRV_Risk_Analysis_AI_adoption_and_trends_in_securities_markets.pdf). The IMF’s 2024 assessment is directionally consistent: AI is being used in investment decisions, execution, and monitoring, but firms still regard having a **human in the loop** as essential for regulatory, risk-management, liability, and ethical reasons [[87]](https://www.imf.org/-/media/files/publications/gfsr/2024/october/english/ch3.pdf).

This matters for the MVO-BL-DL comparison. The relevant question is not which family is theoretically superior in isolation. It is which family is best at:
- **measuring risk**,  
- **predicting returns**,  
- **constructing stable portfolios**, and  
- **operating under realistic market, cost, and governance constraints** [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591).

---

## Conceptual Foundations and Definitions

### The three model families

- **Mean-Variance Optimization (MVO)** chooses weights to maximize expected return for a given variance, minimize variance for a target return, or maximize mean-variance utility, using expected returns \( \mu \) and covariance matrix \( \Sigma \) as core inputs [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf).
- **Black-Litterman (BL)** starts from **equilibrium-implied returns** derived from a reference portfolio and combines them with **views** and **confidence levels** through Bayesian updating before optimizing weights [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf).
- **Deep learning (DL)** is a family of nonlinear models that includes:
  1. **supervised forecasting models** such as MLPs, CNNs, LSTMs, GRUs, and Transformers,  
  2. **reinforcement learning allocators** such as TD3, PPO, A2C, DDPG-style agents, and  
  3. **end-to-end differentiable portfolio models** trained directly on portfolio objectives [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[139]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.09170).

### Mechanism map

| Model family | Risk treatment | Expected return treatment | Allocation decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MVO** | Risk is usually \(w^\top \Sigma w\); variance/covariance is central [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf) | Expected returns are exogenous inputs; MVO itself does not solve forecasting [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[67]](https://users.nber.org/~confer/2006/si2006/ap/uppal.pdf) | Direct optimization of weights subject to constraints [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf) |
| **BL** | Usually covariance-based, with additional posterior uncertainty around views [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf) | Market-implied prior + views + confidence produce posterior expected returns [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html) | Usually a downstream MVO-like optimizer [[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf) |
| **DL** | Flexible: variance, drawdown, CVaR, reward-defined risk, or external risk engine [[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[120]](https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7072/14/3/75)[[132]](https://arxiv.org/html/2604.14206v1) | Learns nonlinear mappings from data to forecasts, states, or actions [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf) | Either downstream optimizer, direct policy, or differentiable weight layer [[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[139]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.09170) |

### Short formula-level outline

**MVO**
\[
\max_{w \in C} \; w^\top \mu - \frac{\lambda}{2} w^\top \Sigma w
\]
where \(w\) are weights, \( \mu \) expected returns, \( \Sigma \) covariance, \( \lambda \) risk aversion, and \(C\) the feasible set [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf).

A common equivalent constrained form is:
\[
\arg \min_w \; w^\top \Sigma w \quad \text{s.t. } w^\top \mu = \mu^*, \; w^\top 1 = 1, \; w \ge 0
\]
for long-only portfolios [[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf).

**Black-Litterman**
\[
\Pi = \delta \Sigma w_{mkt}
\]
\[
E[R] = \left[(\tau\Sigma)^{-1} + P^\top \Omega^{-1} P\right]^{-1}
\left[(\tau\Sigma)^{-1}\Pi + P^\top \Omega^{-1} Q\right]
\]
where \( \Pi \) is the equilibrium prior, \(P\) the picking matrix, \(Q\) the view vector, \( \Omega \) the view-uncertainty matrix, and \( \tau \) scales confidence in the prior [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html).

**Deep learning allocation pipeline**
**Data → representation/sequence model → forecast/regime/policy output → optional uncertainty calibration → optional BL or optimizer layer → weights → execution → monitoring** [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[139]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.09170).

---

## Method for the Review and Source Selection

This report relies on four types of web-accessible evidence:

1. **Foundational and technical finance sources** for MVO and BL mechanics [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf)  
2. **Recent peer-reviewed reviews and systematic surveys** for DL use in asset management and forecasting [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf)  
3. **Institutional, regulatory, and practitioner sources** for deployment constraints and governance [[86]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2025-02/ESMA50-43599798-9923_TRV_Article_Artificial_intelligence_in_EU_investment_funds.pdf)[[88]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2026-02/ESMA50-481369926-30599_TRV_Risk_Analysis_AI_adoption_and_trends_in_securities_markets.pdf)[[87]](https://www.imf.org/-/media/files/publications/gfsr/2024/october/english/ch3.pdf)[[89]](https://www.dechert.com/knowledge/onpoint/2023/9/sec-proposes-new-regulatory-framework-for-use-of-ai-by-broker-de.html)[[145]](https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/financial-stability-publications/fsr/special/html/ecb.fsrart202405_02~58c3ce5246.en.html)[[146]](https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P14112024.pdf)[[150]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2022/october/artificial-intelligence)[[156]](https://www.iif.com/portals/0/Files/content/Innovation/2024%20IIF-EY%20Survey%20Report%20on%20AI_ML%20Use%20in%20Financial%20Services_Public%2001.08.25.pdf)  
4. **Empirical comparative studies** that include out-of-sample testing, transaction costs, regime analysis, or implementation realism [[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf)[[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf)[[103]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/eurjfi/v23y2017i1p1-30.html)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[102]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:758241/FULLTEXT01)

The evidence is strongest on **mechanism and governance**, reasonably strong on **classical model limitations**, and more uneven on **DL head-to-head performance**, where many studies use different objectives, information sets, or cost assumptions [[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591). That source-quality asymmetry is important for the report’s conclusions: claims about hybrid feasibility are better supported than claims that any one standalone DL architecture consistently dominates.

---

## Consolidated Model Comparison Matrix

| Decision dimension | Mean-Variance | Black-Litterman | Deep learning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary role in the stack** | Allocation engine [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf) | Bayesian return regularization + allocation [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf) | Nonlinear forecasting, regime detection, policy learning, or end-to-end allocation [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[139]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.09170) |
| **Key assumption about structure** | Mean and covariance summarize the relevant decision problem, often under normal returns or quadratic utility [[70]](http://boston.qwafafew.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/3/2015/10/revere3.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf) | Market equilibrium is a useful prior; views can be stated linearly with confidence [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf) | Useful patterns are nonlinear, high-dimensional, and learnable from data [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf) |
| **Core inputs** | \( \mu \), \( \Sigma \), constraints [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf) | \( \Sigma \), market weights, priors, views, confidence, constraints [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html) | Prices, features, technicals, macro, sentiment, text, graph relations, reward/loss design [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[126]](https://kronika.ac/wp-content/uploads/19-KKJ2564.pdf)[[147]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.15532v1) |
| **Risk model default** | Variance/covariance [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf) | Covariance + posterior uncertainty in returns [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html) | Flexible: variance, CVaR, drawdown, learned utility, or external risk layer [[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[120]](https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7072/14/3/75)[[132]](https://arxiv.org/html/2604.14206v1) |
| **Return model default** | Exogenous estimate; often fragile sample/factor estimates [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[67]](https://users.nber.org/~confer/2006/si2006/ap/uppal.pdf) | Equilibrium prior + views + confidence [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[53]](https://www.engineeringletters.com/issues_v29/issue_2/EL_29_2_40.pdf) | Learned nonlinear mapping from features to forecasts or actions [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf) |
| **Portfolio construction strength** | Highest transparency and constraint control [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html) | Similar optimizer strength, improved inputs [[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf) | Varies widely; strongest when paired with explicit constraint layers [[139]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.09170)[[143]](https://cvxpylayers.org/) |
| **Interpretability** | High [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf) | Medium [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html) | Low to medium, usually lower than classical models [[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf)[[52]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.14486v1)[[34]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-26337-x) |
| **Subjectivity** | Present but partly hidden in estimation windows and constraints [[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf) | Explicit in views, \( \Omega \), \( \tau \) [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[58]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1800205/FULLTEXT01.pdf)[[75]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:372812/FULLTEXT01.pdf) | Hidden in architecture, labels, reward design, and retraining choices [[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591) |
| **Data requirements** | Moderate [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf) | Moderate [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html) | Often high, especially with alternative data and larger architectures [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[88]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2026-02/ESMA50-481369926-30599_TRV_Risk_Analysis_AI_adoption_and_trends_in_securities_markets.pdf) |
| **Best standalone use case** | Governance-heavy, constraint-heavy portfolios [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[150]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2022/october/artificial-intelligence) | Institutional portfolios with benchmarks and structured views [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[103]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/eurjfi/v23y2017i1p1-30.html) | Data-rich tactical or signal-intensive settings [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[147]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.15532v1) |
| **Main failure mode** | Estimation-error amplification [[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[1]](https://www.andrew.cmu.edu/user/gc0v/webpub/estimationerror.pdf) | Bad or overconfident views; stale priors [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[75]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:372812/FULLTEXT01.pdf) | Overfitting, drift, cost-insensitive overtrading, weak auditability [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[146]](https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P14112024.pdf) |
| **Conditional general-purpose ranking** | Strong if simplicity/governance dominates | Best standalone compromise in many institutional settings | Strongest as upstream module, not usually as standalone final allocator [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591) |

**Tiered recommendation for a general-purpose deployment:**  
**1. Hybrid DL → BL → constrained optimizer**, **2. BL**, **3. regularized/constrained MVO**, **4. standalone DL allocator**—not because DL lacks value, but because predictive flexibility does not by itself guarantee stable, auditable, cost-aware portfolios [[53]](https://www.engineeringletters.com/issues_v29/issue_2/EL_29_2_40.pdf)[[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1)[[121]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.11273)[[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf).

## Mean-Variance Optimization: Core Mechanics, Assumptions, Strengths, and Limitations

### Core mechanics

MVO remains the canonical benchmark because it directly implements the trade-off between expected return and risk. In unconstrained form, the optimizer depends on \( \Sigma^{-1}\mu \), which is why both the expected-return vector and the inverse covariance matrix are so influential in determining weights [[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf). That structure is elegant and fast, but it also explains fragility: when the optimizer sees even slightly better estimated returns for some assets, it tends to treat them as genuine opportunities and allocate aggressively.

### Assumptions

MVO is theoretically exact when either:
- **portfolio returns are normally distributed**, or  
- the investor has **quadratic utility**,  
because then mean and variance are sufficient statistics for the choice problem [[70]](http://boston.qwafafew.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/3/2015/10/revere3.pdf).

Practical implementations usually add further assumptions:
- historical returns are informative for future \( \mu \) and \( \Sigma \),  
- correlation structure is stable enough to estimate, and  
- the sample is rich enough relative to the number of assets to support covariance inversion [[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[67]](https://users.nber.org/~confer/2006/si2006/ap/uppal.pdf).

Each of those assumptions becomes weaker as the asset universe expands or the regime shifts.

### Why the normality assumption often fails in real markets

The normality issue is not a minor technical footnote. Financial returns are repeatedly found to exhibit **fat tails, skewness, and volatility clustering** [[71]](https://d-nb.info/1078505004/34)[[72]](http://aeconf.com/Articles/May2007/aef080102.pdf)[[73]](https://www.cirje.e.u-tokyo.ac.jp/research/dp/2003/2003cf211.pdf). Evidence cited in this review includes:
- broad empirical findings across **66 asset classes** showing skewness and excess kurtosis [[39]](https://www.frontieradvisors.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/Market-Insights-Non-normal-distribution-assumptions-skew-and-kurtosis.pdf), and
- a dynamic BL study in international sectors where returns were **leptokurtic in all 30 series**, with normality rejected in most cases [[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf).

The mechanism matters. Variance penalizes positive and negative deviations symmetrically. But investment risk is usually concentrated in the **left tail**: crashes, drawdowns, forced liquidations, and correlation spikes. A portfolio with infrequent large losses can therefore look acceptable under variance while being economically unattractive once skewness, kurtosis, or drawdown risk is considered [[71]](https://d-nb.info/1078505004/34)[[39]](https://www.frontieradvisors.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/Market-Insights-Non-normal-distribution-assumptions-skew-and-kurtosis.pdf)[[72]](http://aeconf.com/Articles/May2007/aef080102.pdf).

### Strengths

MVO retains real practical advantages.

- **Analytical clarity.** Inputs, constraints, and objective are explicit [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf).  
- **Operational maturity.** Constraint-heavy optimizations are widely solved in production settings [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html).  
- **Governance fit.** Risk committees can inspect position bounds, exposures, and trade-offs more easily than in neural systems [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[150]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2022/october/artificial-intelligence).  
- **Scalability as a decision layer.** Once \( \mu \) and \( \Sigma \) are available, optimization is fast and repeatable [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html).

These are why MVO remains prevalent even after decades of criticism.

### Limitations

The central weakness is **estimation-error amplification**. The optimizer is trying to exploit estimated differences in expected returns, yet expected returns are typically the noisiest inputs in the system [[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[1]](https://www.andrew.cmu.edu/user/gc0v/webpub/estimationerror.pdf). MOSEK’s documentation states the practical consequence plainly: small changes in inputs can generate large changes in the optimal portfolio, including unintuitive positions and high turnover [[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html). Michaud’s characterization of mean-variance optimization as an “estimation-error maximizer” captures the same mechanism [[8]](https://newfrontieradvisors.com/media/rxbld4hq/estimation-error-and-portfolio-optimization-12-05.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf).

That problem is not merely theoretical. DeMiguel, Garlappi, and Uppal found that across **14** portfolio models and **7** datasets, none consistently outperformed the simple **1/N** rule out of sample once estimation error was taken seriously [[67]](https://users.nber.org/~confer/2006/si2006/ap/uppal.pdf). The implication is stark: if expected returns are poorly estimated, optimization can convert noise into concentrated bets.

A second limitation is **high-dimensional covariance instability**. As the number of assets rises, estimating \( \Sigma \) becomes difficult unless the sample is long enough or structure such as sparsity is imposed [[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf). This is especially relevant in FinTech settings with many tradable assets but limited clean history.

A third limitation is **single-period and symmetric-risk framing**. Static MVO does not naturally capture dynamic rebalancing, transaction costs, drawdown objectives, or crisis-specific correlation spikes unless the practitioner adds those elements explicitly [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf).

### Balanced judgment

MVO is still the **cleanest allocation engine** of the three families. It is best treated as a disciplined downstream decision layer, not as a sufficient standalone investment process when return forecasts are noisy, market distributions are non-normal, and implementation frictions are material [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[67]](https://users.nber.org/~confer/2006/si2006/ap/uppal.pdf).

## Black-Litterman: Core Mechanics, Assumptions, Strengths, and Limitations

### Core mechanics

BL was created to address the practical pathology of naïve MVO: unstable expected returns leading to concentrated, unintuitive portfolios [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[12]](https://globalriskguard.com/resources/assetman/assetall_0004.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html). Instead of plugging historical means directly into the optimizer, BL:

1. derives **equilibrium returns** from market-cap or benchmark weights,  
2. expresses investor or model views as linear statements using \(P\) and \(Q\),  
3. attaches uncertainty to those views through \( \Omega \), often alongside the prior-scaling parameter \( \tau \), and  
4. computes posterior expected returns that are then fed into a mean-variance optimizer [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf).

Mechanistically, BL’s portfolio tilt is proportional to the gap between the views and the prior and inversely related to uncertainty. Low-confidence views barely move the posterior. High-confidence views move it more [[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf)[[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf).

### Assumptions

BL assumes:
- the market or benchmark portfolio is a useful anchor,  
- views can be represented in structured linear form, and  
- confidence in views can be quantified well enough to update the prior rationally [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf).

Those assumptions are often more practical than raw sample-mean estimation, but they are not assumption-free. MOSEK notes one specific difficulty: the model often treats views as exogenous relative to historical data, which can be hard to justify in practice if the views were themselves derived from that same data [[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html).

### Strengths

BL’s main strength is **regularization of expected returns**. It shrinks noisy signals toward equilibrium rather than letting each estimated alpha dominate the optimizer [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf). That has several practical effects:

- **more diversified allocations**,  
- **less extreme positions**,  
- **lower turnover**, and  
- **portfolios that are easier to defend institutionally** [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[103]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/eurjfi/v23y2017i1p1-30.html)[[102]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:758241/FULLTEXT01)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf).

The evidence in this review supports those claims, though not uniformly in every sample.

- A global multi-asset study found BL outperforming **1/N, strategic weights, MVO, Bayes-Stein, and minimum-variance** strategies in out-of-sample Sharpe while also showing lower risk, lower turnover, and less extreme allocations [[103]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/eurjfi/v23y2017i1p1-30.html).
- A dynamic BL study using DCC-style covariance and tail-aware reward logic reported better out-of-sample performance than benchmark and equal-weight alternatives, especially when tail risk was handled explicitly [[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf).
- A 2021 German equity study found BL beat the **DAX**, the reference portfolio, and equal-weighting, although it was **slightly outperformed by the MV portfolio** on overall performance; even there, BL still produced lower normalized stock weights and smaller weight fluctuations, which many investors may prefer [[101]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/spt/fininv/v10y2021i2f10_2_2.html).
- A direct MVO-vs-BL study on **23 of the 30** OMXS30 stocks over **January 2003 to December 2013**, rebalanced every **20 trading days**, reported BL outperforming the market-cap benchmark and classical MVO on a risk-adjusted basis in **four out of five** simulated investment-strength settings, while also appearing less sensitive to changes in risk aversion, prior uncertainty, and brokerage assumptions [[102]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:758241/FULLTEXT01).

These studies do not prove BL always wins. They do show a consistent mechanism: **BL often sacrifices some aggressiveness in exchange for weight stability and more moderate bets** [[101]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/spt/fininv/v10y2021i2f10_2_2.html)[[102]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:758241/FULLTEXT01)[[103]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/eurjfi/v23y2017i1p1-30.html).

### Limitations

BL’s major limitation is **subjectivity**. Views must come from somewhere: analysts, committees, macro models, or machine learning systems. The confidence matrix \( \Omega \) and scalar \( \tau \) materially affect the posterior, and the literature does not provide a single agreed calibration rule [[58]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1800205/FULLTEXT01.pdf)[[75]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:372812/FULLTEXT01.pdf)[[76]](https://sites.duke.edu/djepapers/files/2016/10/danielroeder-dje.original.pdf). Idzorek’s contribution—a **0% to 100% confidence** interpretation—makes the model more usable, but it does not eliminate judgment [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf).

The second limitation is that BL usually **inherits MVO’s downstream structure**. It improves the return-estimation layer, but it often still relies on the covariance matrix and a mean-variance-style optimizer [[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf). If covariance estimates are unstable or the equilibrium prior is a poor anchor during structural breaks, BL can still underperform [[69]](https://www.mit.edu/~dbertsim/papers/Finance/Inverse%20Optimization%20-%20A%20New%20Perspective%20on%20the%20Black-Litterman%20Model.pdf)[[75]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:372812/FULLTEXT01.pdf).

The third limitation is that model-generated views can create a false sense of objectivity. If a neural network produces \(Q\), the subjectivity has not disappeared; it has moved into model design, training data, and uncertainty mapping [[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1)[[53]](https://www.engineeringletters.com/issues_v29/issue_2/EL_29_2_40.pdf)[[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf).

### Balanced judgment

BL is best understood as a **Bayesian bridge** between beliefs and optimization. It is usually stronger than raw MVO when the main problem is unstable expected returns. Its advantage erodes when the prior is stale, views are weak, or confidence is poorly calibrated [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf)[[75]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:372812/FULLTEXT01.pdf).

## Deep Learning for Investing: Model Families, Strengths, and Limitations

### Taxonomy: three distinct DL uses in asset allocation

The most important clarification is that “deep learning for investing” refers to at least **three different problem formulations** [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[139]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.09170).

#### 1. Supervised forecasting models
These models predict next-period returns, direction, volatility, or regime labels from historical and auxiliary features. Common architectures include **MLPs, CNNs, LSTMs, GRUs, and Transformers** [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591). In the recent forecasting literature, **RNN/LSTM** models remain the most common, while **CNN-LSTM hybrids** are increasingly used to model joint temporal and cross-sectional structure [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822).

#### 2. Reinforcement learning allocators
These models treat portfolio selection as a sequential decision problem. An agent maps market state to portfolio action to maximize long-run reward, often including risk aversion and transaction cost terms in the reward design [[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf). A representative 2024 study uses **TD3** and embeds both **risk aversion** and **transaction cost constraints** in an extended Markowitz-style reward [[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf).

#### 3. End-to-end differentiable portfolio optimizers
These models bypass the two-stage “forecast then optimize” pipeline and instead map features directly to weights, training on a differentiable portfolio objective such as **Sharpe ratio**, utility, or CVaR [[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf)[[139]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.09170). One line of research constrains outputs via softmax to produce long-only weights that sum to one; later work adds layers for long-only, maximum position, cardinality, leverage, and other portfolio constraints [[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf)[[139]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.09170).

### Why deep learning can add value

DL’s comparative advantage is **nonlinear representation learning**. It can model interactions and dependencies that are difficult to express in linear factor frameworks or analyst view matrices, particularly when signals are:
- high-dimensional,  
- sequential,  
- weak individually but informative jointly, or  
- spread across modalities such as prices, fundamentals, macro releases, text, or sentiment [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[145]](https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/financial-stability-publications/fsr/special/html/ecb.fsrart202405_02~58c3ce5246.en.html).

That is why DL is especially attractive in data-rich tactical allocation and research pipelines.

### Why better forecasting does not automatically mean better portfolios

This is one of the most important causal distinctions in the literature. A model can improve **forecasting loss** without improving the **economic objective** of the portfolio [[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf)[[141]](https://tesi.luiss.it/42702/1/758501_BOSCO_LEONARDO.pdf). For example, reducing mean-squared error on return predictions does not guarantee higher Sharpe, lower drawdown, or lower turnover. That misalignment is one reason end-to-end portfolio methods emerged: they attempt to optimize directly for the investment objective rather than a statistical surrogate [[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf)[[139]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.09170).

### Strengths

- **Highest nonlinear predictive flexibility** among the three families [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)  
- **Native use of alternative and unstructured data** such as text, sentiment, ESG, and macro features [[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[145]](https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/financial-stability-publications/fsr/special/html/ecb.fsrart202405_02~58c3ce5246.en.html)  
- **Potential regime sensitivity** through sequence models and state representations [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)  
- **Direct objective alignment** in end-to-end or RL setups, at least in principle [[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[139]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.09170)

### Limitations

The weaknesses are equally material.

- **Interpretability remains limited.** Even when post hoc tools such as SHAP, LIME, sparse attention, or hierarchical structures are added, explaining why a deep model changed a portfolio is usually harder than explaining BL or MVO [[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf)[[52]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.14486v1)[[34]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-26337-x)[[150]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2022/october/artificial-intelligence).
- **Finance is low signal-to-noise.** That makes overfitting a first-order risk, not a minor implementation detail [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf).
- **Nonstationarity is severe.** Patterns learned in one period may not hold in another, especially through crises or structural breaks [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[145]](https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/financial-stability-publications/fsr/special/html/ecb.fsrart202405_02~58c3ce5246.en.html).
- **Cost sensitivity is often underestimated.** Some end-to-end DL strategies lose much of their advantage once even **2 bps** of transaction costs are included [[2]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/16.4-deep-learning-portfolio-case-studies.html).
- **Benchmarking is often unfair.** The J.P. Morgan/ICAPS comparison notes that many DRL papers benchmark against weak baselines, use different objectives for RL and MVO, or give RL richer information sets than classical models [[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf).

### Balanced judgment

DL is best viewed as a **powerful upstream modeling toolkit** and, in some cases, a tactical policy engine. It is not automatically a superior end-to-end replacement for risk-controlled portfolio construction. Its value is highest when paired with strong downstream regularization, constraints, and monitoring [[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf)[[145]](https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/financial-stability-publications/fsr/special/html/ecb.fsrart202405_02~58c3ce5246.en.html)[[146]](https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P14112024.pdf).

## Comparison I: Risk Measurement Across Model Families

### Core comparison

**MVO** measures risk primarily through **variance/covariance**. That is transparent and computationally convenient, but it compresses all uncertainty into a symmetric second-moment metric [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[71]](https://d-nb.info/1078505004/34).

**BL** usually retains the same covariance backbone but adds **uncertainty about expected returns** through the Bayesian update. This makes BL better than naïve MVO at handling uncertainty in the return-estimation layer, though not necessarily at handling extreme downside risk unless the risk model is extended [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf).

**DL** is the most flexible. It can use variance, expected shortfall, drawdown, custom reward penalties, regime-dependent risk, or an external risk engine [[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[120]](https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7072/14/3/75)[[132]](https://arxiv.org/html/2604.14206v1). But that flexibility comes with a trade-off: if the risk objective is embedded inside a complex neural reward, it can be harder to validate and stress test than explicit variance or scenario constraints [[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf)[[150]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2022/october/artificial-intelligence).

### Risk measurement comparison table

| Dimension | Mean-Variance | Black-Litterman | Deep learning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Base risk measure** | Variance / covariance [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf) | Covariance + posterior uncertainty in expected returns [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html) | Varies: variance, drawdown, CVaR, reward-defined risk, or external risk engine [[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[120]](https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7072/14/3/75)[[132]](https://arxiv.org/html/2604.14206v1) |
| **Tail risk handling** | Weak unless extended [[71]](https://d-nb.info/1078505004/34)[[39]](https://www.frontieradvisors.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/Market-Insights-Non-normal-distribution-assumptions-skew-and-kurtosis.pdf) | Weak in baseline; stronger in tail-aware extensions [[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf) | Potentially strong if trained on drawdown/CVaR-aware objectives [[120]](https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7072/14/3/75)[[132]](https://arxiv.org/html/2604.14206v1) |
| **Regime sensitivity** | Low in static form [[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[67]](https://users.nber.org/~confer/2006/si2006/ap/uppal.pdf) | Medium if views or covariances are regime-aware [[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf)[[54]](https://www.cse.wustl.edu/~yixin.chen/public/Allocation.pdf) | Potentially high, especially in sequential or RL systems [[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[147]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.15532v1) |
| **Stress testing compatibility** | High because inputs and constraints are explicit [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[150]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2022/october/artificial-intelligence) | High because scenarios can be encoded as views [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf) | Medium; possible but harder to audit [[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf)[[145]](https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/financial-stability-publications/fsr/special/html/ecb.fsrart202405_02~58c3ce5246.en.html) |
| **Robustness to input error** | Low without shrinkage/constraints [[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf) | Better than naïve MVO on the return side [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[103]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/eurjfi/v23y2017i1p1-30.html) | Highly variable; can overfit or drift [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591) |
| **Interpretability of risk logic** | High [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf) | Medium [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html) | Low to medium [[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf)[[52]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.14486v1) |

### Analytical bottom line on risk

- **Best for transparent, auditable risk accounting:** MVO  
- **Best for uncertainty-aware return regularization:** BL  
- **Best for flexible downside-aware objective design:** DL  

But none alone is ideal when the requirement is simultaneously **tail sensitivity, regime awareness, and governance transparency**. That is a major reason hybrids are attractive [[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf)[[120]](https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7072/14/3/75)[[132]](https://arxiv.org/html/2604.14206v1)[[145]](https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/financial-stability-publications/fsr/special/html/ecb.fsrart202405_02~58c3ce5246.en.html).

## Comparison II: Return Prediction Across Model Families

### Core comparison

Return prediction is where the model families differ most sharply.

**MVO** has almost no native return-formation logic. If the practitioner uses historical means, analyst forecasts, or factor estimates, MVO treats them as point inputs. That is exactly why it is so fragile: it turns noisy expected returns into large bets [[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[67]](https://users.nber.org/~confer/2006/si2006/ap/uppal.pdf).

**BL** improves this by **shrinking views toward an equilibrium prior**. It does not eliminate forecast error, but it regularizes it. The practical question becomes not “What is the sample mean?” but “How far should I move away from the benchmark, and how confident am I?” [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf).

**DL** has the broadest predictive capacity. It can learn nonlinear relationships, cross-asset dependencies, and temporal patterns from richer information sets [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591). But predictive strength is meaningful only if forecasts are **well calibrated** and translated into stable positions.

### Return prediction comparison table

| Return source / method | Mean-Variance | Black-Litterman | Deep learning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Historical/sample means** | Common, but unstable [[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[67]](https://users.nber.org/~confer/2006/si2006/ap/uppal.pdf) | Not the baseline [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html) | Usually replaced by learned forecasts [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf) |
| **Equilibrium-implied returns** | Not native [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf) | Core prior [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html) | Can be used externally in hybrids [[44]](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.14345v2)[[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1) |
| **Subjective views** | Implicit through analyst overrides or signal design [[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html) | Explicit via \(P, Q, \Omega, \tau\) [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[58]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1800205/FULLTEXT01.pdf) | Often implicit in architecture, labels, and reward design [[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591) |
| **Factor signals** | Common upstream input [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[67]](https://users.nber.org/~confer/2006/si2006/ap/uppal.pdf) | Can enter as structured views [[53]](https://www.engineeringletters.com/issues_v29/issue_2/EL_29_2_40.pdf)[[54]](https://www.cse.wustl.edu/~yixin.chen/public/Allocation.pdf) | Common features or latent factors [[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[124]](https://wp.lancs.ac.uk/fofi2022/files/2022/08/FoFI-2022-007-Iason-Kynigakis.pdf) |
| **Sequence models** | No | Only indirectly, if they generate views [[53]](https://www.engineeringletters.com/issues_v29/issue_2/EL_29_2_40.pdf)[[44]](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.14345v2) | Yes: LSTM/GRU/Transformer [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf) |
| **Alternative data** | Weak native fit [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf) | Possible through view construction [[44]](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.14345v2)[[53]](https://www.engineeringletters.com/issues_v29/issue_2/EL_29_2_40.pdf) | Native strength [[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[145]](https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/financial-stability-publications/fsr/special/html/ecb.fsrart202405_02~58c3ce5246.en.html) |

### Worked example: how a DL forecast becomes a BL view

A useful way to compare return modeling is to run one concrete hybrid example.

Suppose a sequence model produces the following tactical signal:  
**Asset 2 is expected to outperform Asset 1 by 9 percentage points over the next horizon**, with forecast uncertainty of **3 percentage points** [[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1).

A BL-compatible encoding is:
- \(P = [-1, 1, 0, \dots, 0]\)  
- \(Q = 0.09\)  
- \( \Omega_{ii} = 0.03^2 \) [[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1)

Interpretation:
- The row of \(P\) defines a **relative view**: long Asset 2, short Asset 1.  
- \(Q = 0.09\) says the expected spread is **+9%**.  
- \( \Omega_{ii} = 0.03^2 \) says the uncertainty around that spread is **3% standard deviation** [[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1).

Now combine that with the equilibrium prior:
\[
\Pi = \delta \Sigma w_{mkt}
\]
where one applied study uses \( \delta = 2.5 \) and \( \tau = 1 \) as concrete implementation choices [[54]](https://www.cse.wustl.edu/~yixin.chen/public/Allocation.pdf).

**Intermediate step:** the posterior return for Assets 1 and 2 does **not** jump fully to the raw DL forecast. It moves **part of the way** from the prior toward the view, with the size of the tilt determined by confidence [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf).

**Output:** the optimizer then increases weight in Asset 2 and reduces weight in Asset 1, but by **less** than a naive forecast-to-weight rule would imply, because BL shrinks the forecast toward equilibrium. If the forecast weakens or confidence falls, the tilt shrinks automatically [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf)[[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1).

This example shows why BL is a natural bridge between ML outputs and portfolio construction: it turns raw predictive scores into **auditable, confidence-weighted allocation inputs** rather than treating them as certainties [[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf)[[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1).

### Bottom line on return prediction

- **Weakest predictive framework:** MVO  
- **Best regularized return formation:** BL  
- **Best nonlinear predictive capacity:** DL  

The key caution is that **predictive flexibility and portfolio robustness are different properties**. DL can dominate on representation learning and still fail at investable decision-making if uncertainty and turnover are poorly controlled [[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf)[[141]](https://tesi.luiss.it/42702/1/758501_BOSCO_LEONARDO.pdf)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591).

## Comparison III: Portfolio Construction and Rebalancing

### Core comparison

On portfolio construction, **MVO remains the strongest pure decision layer** because it is explicitly designed to map beliefs and constraints into weights [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf). That matters operationally: long-only constraints, leverage limits, exposure budgets, transaction costs, and turnover penalties fit naturally into convex or near-convex optimization frameworks [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html)[[121]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.11273).

**BL** normally inherits the same construction machinery. Its portfolio benefit is indirect but important: by improving return inputs, it often yields lower turnover and less concentration even without changing the optimizer [[102]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:758241/FULLTEXT01)[[103]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/eurjfi/v23y2017i1p1-30.html).

**DL** spans three cases:
- forecast then optimize,  
- direct policy learning via RL, and  
- end-to-end differentiable weight generation [[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[139]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.09170).

The main distinction is control. Classical optimization makes constraints and costs explicit. DL can incorporate them, but often with more implementation complexity and weaker auditability [[139]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.09170)[[143]](https://cvxpylayers.org/)[[146]](https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P14112024.pdf).

### Portfolio construction comparison table

| Dimension | Mean-Variance | Black-Litterman | Deep learning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Optimization objective** | Utility / variance / Sharpe-type trade-off [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf) | Usually same downstream objective, with posterior inputs [[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf) | Forecast loss, Sharpe, utility, drawdown, or RL reward [[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[139]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.09170) |
| **Constraint handling** | Strong and explicit [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html) | Strong via same optimizer [[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html) | Possible, but often harder; differentiable layers help [[139]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.09170)[[143]](https://cvxpylayers.org/) |
| **Turnover control** | Must be added explicitly [[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html)[[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf) | Often lower than naïve MVO because inputs are more stable [[102]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:758241/FULLTEXT01)[[103]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/eurjfi/v23y2017i1p1-30.html) | Can be penalized directly, but often omitted or under-modeled [[2]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/16.4-deep-learning-portfolio-case-studies.html)[[126]](https://kronika.ac/wp-content/uploads/19-KKJ2564.pdf) |
| **Transaction costs** | Explicitly modelable [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html) | Explicitly modelable [[103]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/eurjfi/v23y2017i1p1-30.html) | Possible in RL/end-to-end systems; often decisive [[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[2]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/16.4-deep-learning-portfolio-case-studies.html) |
| **Scalability** | High once inputs are estimated [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf) | High [[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html) | Variable; training can be computationally heavy [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[148]](https://openreview.net/pdf/1642ffb5db2aed311cb819e8593f578a35e45a15.pdf) |
| **Rebalancing logic** | Periodic optimization [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf) | Periodic optimization with posterior updates [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html) | Forecast-driven, policy-driven, or end-to-end dynamic [[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[139]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.09170)[[126]](https://kronika.ac/wp-content/uploads/19-KKJ2564.pdf) |

### Why portfolio construction is where classical methods still dominate in practice

The reason is not nostalgia. It is **control over implementation frictions**. A portfolio with strong gross alpha but unstable weights, high turnover, poor liquidity awareness, or opaque constraint interactions is not operationally superior. Classical optimizers still score highly because they let firms specify:
- long-only or leverage bounds,  
- sector or factor exposures,  
- turnover budgets,  
- transaction costs, and  
- benchmark-relative or liability-aware constraints [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf)[[121]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.11273).

That is why many DL systems still end in a classical optimizer rather than replacing it [[121]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.11273)[[143]](https://cvxpylayers.org/)[[156]](https://www.iif.com/portals/0/Files/content/Innovation/2024%20IIF-EY%20Survey%20Report%20on%20AI_ML%20Use%20in%20Financial%20Services_Public%2001.08.25.pdf).

### Bottom line on portfolio construction

- **Best transparency and control:** MVO  
- **Best stabilized version of mean-variance construction:** BL  
- **Best potential for dynamic adaptation:** DL/RL  

But in most institutional and FinTech deployments, **a classical constraint layer remains valuable even when the forecasting layer is learned** [[121]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.11273)[[143]](https://cvxpylayers.org/)[[156]](https://www.iif.com/portals/0/Files/content/Innovation/2024%20IIF-EY%20Survey%20Report%20on%20AI_ML%20Use%20in%20Financial%20Services_Public%2001.08.25.pdf).
