## Performance Under Different Market Conditions and Regimes

### Market condition suitability table

| Market condition | Mean-Variance | Black-Litterman | Deep learning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bull markets** | Can work if means/covariances are reasonably stable [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf) | Works well if priors and views align with trend [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[103]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/eurjfi/v23y2017i1p1-30.html) | Can capture continuation and nonlinear momentum, but may overtrade [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf) |
| **Bear markets** | Often weak if historical estimates lag regime shifts [[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[67]](https://users.nber.org/~confer/2006/si2006/ap/uppal.pdf) | Better if defensive views or updated priors are used [[54]](https://www.cse.wustl.edu/~yixin.chen/public/Allocation.pdf)[[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf) | Potentially strong in adaptive setups [[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[109]](http://www.columbia.edu/~xz2574/download/HJZ-MV.pdf) |
| **Crises / stress** | Vulnerable when correlations spike and tails dominate [[71]](https://d-nb.info/1078505004/34)[[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf) | Dynamic/tail-aware BL can outperform static MV [[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf) | Some RL/dynamic DL systems do better in crises, but evidence is mixed and validation-sensitive [[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf)[[109]](http://www.columbia.edu/~xz2574/download/HJZ-MV.pdf)[[94]](https://communities.springernature.com/posts/building-a-machine-learning-framework-for-dynamic-risk-based-asset-allocation) |
| **High-volatility regimes** | Static covariance estimates degrade [[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[37]](https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7390/13/20/3265) | State-dependent BL can help [[37]](https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7390/13/20/3265)[[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf) | Often the strongest relative use case if explicitly regime-aware [[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[147]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.15532v1)[[94]](https://communities.springernature.com/posts/building-a-machine-learning-framework-for-dynamic-risk-based-asset-allocation) |
| **Structural breaks** | Weak without re-estimation or robustification [[67]](https://users.nber.org/~confer/2006/si2006/ap/uppal.pdf)[[105]](https://digitalcommons.georgefox.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi) | Better than naïve MV if priors anchor noise, but can still lag [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[75]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:372812/FULLTEXT01.pdf) | Potential advantage, but also high drift risk [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[145]](https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/financial-stability-publications/fsr/special/html/ecb.fsrart202405_02~58c3ce5246.en.html) |
| **Low-signal environments** | Often loses to simple rules when means are noisy [[67]](https://users.nber.org/~confer/2006/si2006/ap/uppal.pdf) | Often preferable to naïve MV because of shrinkage [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[103]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/eurjfi/v23y2017i1p1-30.html) | Often disappointing unless data richness is genuinely high [[99]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40854-026-00927-8)[[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822) |

### What the evidence actually says

The empirical literature does **not** support a universal winner.

#### Mean-Variance
MVO tends to look better when:
- the universe is not too high-dimensional,
- return and covariance estimates are reasonably stable,
- constraints are strong enough to prevent corner solutions,
- and governance simplicity is a first-order objective [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html).

It tends to look worse in crises, low-signal settings, or when sample means are used naively [[67]](https://users.nber.org/~confer/2006/si2006/ap/uppal.pdf)[[71]](https://d-nb.info/1078505004/34).

#### Black-Litterman
BL’s strongest relative performance appears when:
- expected returns are noisy,
- the benchmark prior is meaningful,
- the investor has disciplined views,
- and turnover matters [[103]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/eurjfi/v23y2017i1p1-30.html)[[102]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:758241/FULLTEXT01)[[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf).

Its weaker cases are those where:
- the prior is stale,
- view quality is poor,
- or the portfolio universe experiences a structural break not well reflected in either the prior or the views [[69]](https://www.mit.edu/~dbertsim/papers/Finance/Inverse%20Optimization%20-%20A%20New%20Perspective%20on%20the%20Black-Litterman%20Model.pdf)[[75]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:372812/FULLTEXT01.pdf).

#### Deep learning
DL tends to be most promising when:
- data are rich and heterogeneous,
- nonlinear temporal structure exists,
- the model is explicitly cost-aware,
- and the evaluation protocol is walk-forward and realistic [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf).

Its weaker cases include:
- low-dimensional ETF problems where simpler regularized models can outperform neural networks [[99]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40854-026-00927-8),
- strategies where transaction costs are omitted or understated [[2]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/16.4-deep-learning-portfolio-case-studies.html)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf),
- and comparisons where RL is evaluated against unrealistic baselines [[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf).

### Two concrete regime-aware case studies

#### Case 1: Dynamic BL under non-normal sector returns
In one international sector study, returns were **leptokurtic in all 30 series**, with normality rejected in most cases [[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf). That is precisely the environment where plain MVO’s distributional assumptions are weakest. A dynamic BL approach with DCC covariance and tail-aware design outperformed benchmark and naive portfolios [[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf). The causal point is clear: when tails and correlation dynamics matter, improving both the prior/update logic and the risk model can materially help.

#### Case 2: Fair DRL vs MVO benchmarking
The J.P. Morgan/ICAPS 2023 comparison is important less for a single headline result than for its methodology. It aligned the objective function for both DRL and MVO around **Sharpe-ratio maximization** and criticized much of the prior literature for comparing RL against weak baselines or unequal information sets [[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf). This matters because claimed DL superiority is often an artifact of benchmark design rather than a robust economic advantage.

### Main synthesis

- **MVO** is best under stable, governance-heavy conditions.  
- **BL** is often best when expected-return uncertainty is the main weakness.  
- **DL** is most attractive when markets are complex, data-rich, and regime-varying—but only when cost and drift are handled explicitly [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf)[[103]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/eurjfi/v23y2017i1p1-30.html).

## Interpretability, Subjectivity, Robustness, and Other Modeling Trade-offs

### Comparative trade-off summary

| Trade-off axis | Strongest | Middle | Weakest |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Interpretability / auditability** | MVO [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf) | BL [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html) | DL [[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf)[[52]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.14486v1) |
| **Predictive flexibility** | DL [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591) | BL [[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[53]](https://www.engineeringletters.com/issues_v29/issue_2/EL_29_2_40.pdf) | MVO [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[67]](https://users.nber.org/~confer/2006/si2006/ap/uppal.pdf) |
| **Explicitness of subjectivity** | BL [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[58]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1800205/FULLTEXT01.pdf) | MVO [[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf) | DL often hides it in design choices [[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591) |
| **Out-of-the-box robustness to noisy expected returns** | BL [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[103]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/eurjfi/v23y2017i1p1-30.html) | constrained MVO [[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html)[[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf) | standalone DL varies widely [[2]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/16.4-deep-learning-portfolio-case-studies.html)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591) |
| **Operational simplicity** | MVO [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[150]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2022/october/artificial-intelligence) | BL [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf) | DL [[146]](https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P14112024.pdf)[[156]](https://www.iif.com/portals/0/Files/content/Innovation/2024%20IIF-EY%20Survey%20Report%20on%20AI_ML%20Use%20in%20Financial%20Services_Public%2001.08.25.pdf) |

### Why “objective” classical models still contain judgment

MVO is often described as objective because its math is clean. But its outputs depend heavily on subjective choices about:
- estimation windows,
- shrinkage methods,
- constraints,
- rebalance frequency,
- transaction cost assumptions,
- and even the benchmark used to define relative risk [[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf).

BL is often superior here not because it removes subjectivity, but because it **makes it explicit**. Views and confidence can be inspected, debated, and adjusted [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[75]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:372812/FULLTEXT01.pdf).

DL is frequently marketed as purely data-driven, but it embeds subjectivity in:
- feature sets,
- data cleaning,
- target definition,
- model architecture,
- reward design,
- hyperparameter tuning,
- retraining schedule, and
- how outputs are mapped to trades [[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf).

### Why DL interpretability remains a real limitation

The interpretability problem is not merely that deep networks are complex. It is that in finance, explainability is tied to **fiduciary defensibility**. A risk committee must be able to understand why the portfolio changed, why exposures rose, and why the model should still be trusted after a regime change [[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf)[[150]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2022/october/artificial-intelligence)[[156]](https://www.iif.com/portals/0/Files/content/Innovation/2024%20IIF-EY%20Survey%20Report%20on%20AI_ML%20Use%20in%20Financial%20Services_Public%2001.08.25.pdf). Post hoc explanation tools can help with feature importance, but they do not fully solve the problem of explaining a portfolio decision path in an RL policy network or a high-dimensional end-to-end allocator [[52]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.14486v1)[[34]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-26337-x)[[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf).

### Governance and regulatory implications

The regulatory direction reinforces the value of explicit controls.

- The IMF reports that market participants still view a **human in the loop** as essential in AI-based investment processes [[87]](https://www.imf.org/-/media/files/publications/gfsr/2024/october/english/ch3.pdf).
- In **July 2023**, the SEC proposed rules targeting conflicts of interest tied to “predictive data analytics” and covered technologies including **machine learning, deep learning, neural networks, NLP, and GPT systems**; the proposal emphasized testing what factors models optimize and checking for **drift** or **decay** [[89]](https://www.dechert.com/knowledge/onpoint/2023/9/sec-proposes-new-regulatory-framework-for-use-of-ai-by-broker-de.html).
- More broadly, current governance expectations emphasize validation, ongoing monitoring, data quality, documentation, and accountability in investor-facing or risk-critical settings [[145]](https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/financial-stability-publications/fsr/special/html/ecb.fsrart202405_02~58c3ce5246.en.html)[[146]](https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P14112024.pdf)[[150]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2022/october/artificial-intelligence)[[156]](https://www.iif.com/portals/0/Files/content/Innovation/2024%20IIF-EY%20Survey%20Report%20on%20AI_ML%20Use%20in%20Financial%20Services_Public%2001.08.25.pdf).

These requirements increase the practical value of architectures in which **AI informs the process but does not wholly obscure it** [[86]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2025-02/ESMA50-43599798-9923_TRV_Article_Artificial_intelligence_in_EU_investment_funds.pdf)[[87]](https://www.imf.org/-/media/files/publications/gfsr/2024/october/english/ch3.pdf)[[89]](https://www.dechert.com/knowledge/onpoint/2023/9/sec-proposes-new-regulatory-framework-for-use-of-ai-by-broker-de.html).

### Synthesis positioning

- **MVO**: highest interpretability, lowest predictive flexibility  
- **BL**: intermediate interpretability, explicit subjectivity, stronger decision robustness than naïve MVO  
- **DL**: highest flexibility, lowest transparency, highest infrastructure and governance burden

That is the clearest reason no standalone family dominates.

## Can the Models Be Combined? Design of a Hybrid General-Purpose Framework

### Direct answer

Yes. The evidence supports combination not as a loose aspiration but as a **modular architecture** with clear division of labor [100][53][121][122].

### Design principle

Assign each family the task it does relatively best:

- **DL** for nonlinear signal extraction, representation learning, and regime detection [18][32][145]  
- **BL/Bayesian updating** for converting uncertain signals into posterior expected returns with explicit confidence weighting [100][122][53]  
- **classical optimization** for diversification, turnover control, risk budgets, transaction costs, and governance [49][121][123]

### Visual conceptual framework: layered hybrid architecture

```text
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 HYBRID FINTECH ALLOCATION STACK             │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 1. Data Layer                                               │
│    Prices • volumes • fundamentals • macro • text/sentiment │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 2. Representation / Signal Layer                            │
│    LSTM • CNN • Transformer • GNN • regime classifier       │
│    Outputs: forecasts, latent factors, regime probabilities │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 3. Uncertainty & View Translation Layer                     │
│    Ensemble dispersion / Bayesian calibration / mapping     │
│    Outputs: BL-style Q (views) and Ω (confidence)           │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 4. Bayesian Fusion Layer                                    │
│    Π = δΣw_mkt  +  (Q, Ω, P, τ)  → posterior returns        │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 5. Risk Engine                                              │
│    Shrinkage/EWMA/factor covariance + tail-risk overlays    │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 6. Portfolio Construction Layer                             │
│    Constrained MVO / CVaR / risk-budgeting / multi-period   │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 7. Execution, Monitoring, Governance                        │
│    Trade sizing • costs • drift • override • documentation  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Hybrid framework design table

| Module | Role | Candidate methods | Strength contributed | Main failure modes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data ingestion** | Build unified state dataset | Prices, volumes, factors, macro, text [126][145] | Richer information set | Data-quality errors, lag mismatch, vendor dependence [145][146] |
| **Signal generation** | Learn nonlinear return/regime structure | LSTM, Transformer, GNN, boosted trees [18][147][120] | Predictive flexibility | Overfitting, drift, weak calibration [18][32] |
| **Uncertainty calibration** | Estimate forecast confidence | Bayesian NN, ensembles, dropout-style uncertainty [130][131] | Avoid overconfident views | Stress-period miscalibration [130][132] |
| **View translation** | Convert forecasts into structured views | ML-generated \(Q\), \( \Omega \) [53][122] | Makes signals auditable and BL-compatible | Mapping errors, false confidence [11][75] |
| **Bayesian fusion** | Blend prior and views | BL / latent BL [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf) | Return regularization, weight stability | Poor \( \tau/\Omega \) choices, stale prior [[58]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1800205/FULLTEXT01.pdf)[[75]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:372812/FULLTEXT01.pdf) |
| **Risk engine** | Estimate covariance and downside risk | EWMA, shrinkage, factor models, covariance cleaning, CVaR [[123]](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.01918v3)[[124]](https://wp.lancs.ac.uk/fofi2022/files/2022/08/FoFI-2022-007-Iason-Kynigakis.pdf)[[132]](https://arxiv.org/html/2604.14206v1) | More stable risk estimates | Correlation breakdown, misspecification [[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[123]](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.01918v3) |
| **Optimization** | Convert beliefs into feasible portfolio | Constrained MVO, CVaR, risk budgeting, multi-period optimizer [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[121]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.11273)[[132]](https://arxiv.org/html/2604.14206v1) | Implementation discipline | Weak signals can still be amplified [[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html)[[121]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.11273) |
| **Execution/monitoring** | Manage real-world frictions and drift | Cost model, threshold rebalancing, dashboards, retraining [[123]](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.01918v3)[[126]](https://kronika.ac/wp-content/uploads/19-KKJ2564.pdf)[[134]](https://ijsrcseit.com/paper/CSEIT23906203.pdf) | Practical deployability | Operational complexity, monitoring failure [[146]](https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P14112024.pdf)[[156]](https://www.iif.com/portals/0/Files/content/Innovation/2024%20IIF-EY%20Survey%20Report%20on%20AI_ML%20Use%20in%20Financial%20Services_Public%2001.08.25.pdf) |

### Why this is more general-purpose than any standalone model

The hybrid works because it attacks the **specific failure modes** of each family.

- It reduces **MVO fragility** by not feeding raw sample means directly into the optimizer [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf).  
- It reduces **BL subjectivity** by sourcing views from systematic models rather than purely discretionary committee judgments, while still making confidence explicit [[53]](https://www.engineeringletters.com/issues_v29/issue_2/EL_29_2_40.pdf)[[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1).  
- It reduces **DL deployment risk** by not allowing a black-box model to own the entire capital-allocation decision without explicit constraints, risk controls, and audit trails [[121]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.11273)[[156]](https://www.iif.com/portals/0/Files/content/Innovation/2024%20IIF-EY%20Survey%20Report%20on%20AI_ML%20Use%20in%20Financial%20Services_Public%2001.08.25.pdf).

### Worked end-to-end example

A concrete implementation can be described in six steps.

1. **Signal generation:** a Transformer or LSTM predicts cross-asset return scores and regime probabilities from price, macro, and sentiment data [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[145]](https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/financial-stability-publications/fsr/special/html/ecb.fsrart202405_02~58c3ce5246.en.html).  
2. **Uncertainty extraction:** ensemble dispersion or Bayesian calibration is used to estimate forecast confidence [[130]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2308.01222)[[131]](https://cris.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/publications/quantifying-uncertainty-of-portfolios-using-bayesian-neural-netwo/).  
3. **View creation:** the strongest relative signal becomes a BL view, such as  
   \(P=[-1,1,0,\dots]\), \(Q=0.09\), \( \Omega=0.03^2 \) [[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1).  
4. **Bayesian shrinkage:** combine that view with  
   \( \Pi=\delta\Sigma w_{mkt} \), with a practical parameterization such as \( \delta=2.5 \), \( \tau=1 \) from one applied BL study [[54]](https://www.cse.wustl.edu/~yixin.chen/public/Allocation.pdf).  
5. **Portfolio optimization:** solve a long-only constrained problem with turnover and transaction-cost penalties [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[121]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.11273).  
6. **Monitoring:** if realized forecast error rises, regime probabilities shift, or turnover breaches limits, reduce confidence or shrink the portfolio back toward the benchmark [89][134][156].

**Result:** instead of a raw ML signal directly determining large trades, the system produces a **controlled tilt** away from equilibrium, scaled by both model confidence and implementation constraints [100][122][121].

## Implementation Blueprint for a Hybrid FinTech Allocation Stack

### Workflow diagram

```text
Data ingestion
   ↓
Cleaning, lag alignment, feature engineering
   ↓
DL models
   ├─ Return forecasts
   ├─ Regime probabilities
   └─ Uncertainty estimates
   ↓
Translate outputs into BL-style views (Q) and confidences (Ω)
   ↓
Blend with equilibrium prior (Π = δΣw_mkt)
   ↓
Posterior expected returns
   ↓
Risk engine
   ├─ covariance shrinkage / EWMA / factor model
   └─ tail-risk overlay / stress scenarios
   ↓
Constrained optimizer
   ├─ long-only / leverage / exposure limits
   ├─ turnover penalty
   └─ transaction-cost model
   ↓
Execution
   ↓
Monitoring and governance
   ├─ Sharpe / drawdown / CVaR / turnover
   ├─ drift detection
   ├─ attribution
   └─ human review / override / retraining
```

### Practical implementation rules

1. **Separate forecast confidence from forecast magnitude.** A high predicted return with low confidence should not create a large tilt [[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1)[[130]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2308.01222).  
2. **Do not let the BL layer hide weak signals.** If the same unstable model produces both \(Q\) and overly tight \( \Omega \), the posterior can still be distorted [[75]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:372812/FULLTEXT01.pdf)[[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1).  
3. **Keep constraints outside the neural network when possible.** This improves auditability and operational control [[121]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.11273)[[156]](https://www.iif.com/portals/0/Files/content/Innovation/2024%20IIF-EY%20Survey%20Report%20on%20AI_ML%20Use%20in%20Financial%20Services_Public%2001.08.25.pdf).  
4. **Use regime-aware de-risking.** If market conditions move into a stress state, shrink active risk even if the forecasting model remains confident [[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf)[[87]](https://www.imf.org/-/media/files/publications/gfsr/2024/october/english/ch3.pdf).  
5. **Build override paths.** Hybrid systems should support human intervention without breaking audit trails [[87]](https://www.imf.org/-/media/files/publications/gfsr/2024/october/english/ch3.pdf)[[89]](https://www.dechert.com/knowledge/onpoint/2023/9/sec-proposes-new-regulatory-framework-for-use-of-ai-by-broker-de.html)[[150]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2022/october/artificial-intelligence).

### Failure modes to monitor

A hybrid is not automatically safer. It can fail through:
- **forecast-confidence mismatch**,  
- **stale priors during structural breaks**,  
- **correlation spikes that invalidate the risk engine**,  
- **optimizer amplification of weak posteriors**, and  
- **operational complexity that exceeds governance capacity** [[69]](https://www.mit.edu/~dbertsim/papers/Finance/Inverse%20Optimization%20-%20A%20New%20Perspective%20on%20the%20Black-Litterman%20Model.pdf)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html)[[146]](https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P14112024.pdf)[[150]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2022/october/artificial-intelligence).

## Validation, Benchmarking, and Practical Deployment Considerations

### Benchmark evaluation framework

| Component | Recommended practice | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Datasets** | Use multiple universes: ETFs, equities, sector indices, futures [[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf)[[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf)[[2]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/16.4-deep-learning-portfolio-case-studies.html)[[23]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12364330/) | Model performance is universe-dependent [[99]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40854-026-00927-8)[[103]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/eurjfi/v23y2017i1p1-30.html) |
| **Splits** | Walk-forward or expanding windows, not random k-fold [[112]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/8.4-backtesting-market-data.html)[[113]](https://palomar.home.ece.ust.hk/MAFS5310_lectures/slides_backtesting.pdf) | Preserves chronology and reduces leakage [[112]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/8.4-backtesting-market-data.html)[[113]](https://palomar.home.ece.ust.hk/MAFS5310_lectures/slides_backtesting.pdf) |
| **Validation** | Separate train/validation/test and control hyperparameter search [[112]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/8.4-backtesting-market-data.html)[[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf) | Avoids hidden test-set overfitting [[2]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/16.4-deep-learning-portfolio-case-studies.html)[[112]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/8.4-backtesting-market-data.html) |
| **Leakage control** | Lag macro/fundamental inputs to publication availability; purge overlaps where needed [[54]](https://www.cse.wustl.edu/~yixin.chen/public/Allocation.pdf)[[113]](https://palomar.home.ece.ust.hk/MAFS5310_lectures/slides_backtesting.pdf) | Time misalignment can create false alpha [[2]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/16.4-deep-learning-portfolio-case-studies.html)[[113]](https://palomar.home.ece.ust.hk/MAFS5310_lectures/slides_backtesting.pdf) |
| **Transaction costs** | Include explicit cost model and turnover slippage [[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf)[[126]](https://kronika.ac/wp-content/uploads/19-KKJ2564.pdf)[[123]](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.01918v3) | Many DL gains weaken materially after costs [[2]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/16.4-deep-learning-portfolio-case-studies.html)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf) |
| **Metrics** | Report return, Sharpe, Sortino, max drawdown, CVaR/VaR, turnover, stability [[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf)[[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf)[[112]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/8.4-backtesting-market-data.html) | Single-metric claims are often misleading [[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf)[[112]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/8.4-backtesting-market-data.html) |
| **Benchmarks** | Include market, 1/N, constrained MVO, BL, and cost-aware alternatives [[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf)[[103]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/eurjfi/v23y2017i1p1-30.html)[[107]](http://www.efmaefm.org/0EFMAMEETINGS/EFMA%20ANNUAL%20MEETINGS/2023-UK/papers/EFMA%202023_stage-4455_question-Full%20Paper_id-352.pdf) | Weak baselines inflate model claims [[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf) |
| **Robustness** | Test crises, high-volatility regimes, low-signal periods, and multiple seeds [[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf)[[108]](https://lup.lub.lu.se/student-papers/record/9178260/file/9178261.pdf)[[112]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/8.4-backtesting-market-data.html) | Finance is regime-dependent and stochastic [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf) |

### What a fair benchmark must control

The J.P. Morgan/ICAPS comparison makes a methodological point that should become standard: **align the economic objective and the information set across model classes** [[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf). Comparing DRL trained on one reward against MVO optimized on another, or giving one model news or sentiment inputs that the benchmark does not have, is not a clean horse race [[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf).

### Practical deployment lessons

1. **Transaction costs are first-order.** Gross backtest alpha is not enough; some reported DL advantages shrink sharply once even **2 bps** of costs are added [[2]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/16.4-deep-learning-portfolio-case-studies.html).  
2. **Turnover must be designed, not hoped for.** BL often helps because more stable return inputs produce less extreme reallocations; RL and end-to-end systems need explicit turnover penalties [[102]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:758241/FULLTEXT01)[[126]](https://kronika.ac/wp-content/uploads/19-KKJ2564.pdf)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf).  
3. **Chronology-respecting validation is mandatory.** Random splits are not credible for portfolio models [[112]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/8.4-backtesting-market-data.html)[[113]](https://palomar.home.ece.ust.hk/MAFS5310_lectures/slides_backtesting.pdf).  
4. **Monitoring continues after go-live.** Drift, data freshness, and changing market microstructure can invalidate a once-successful model [[89]](https://www.dechert.com/knowledge/onpoint/2023/9/sec-proposes-new-regulatory-framework-for-use-of-ai-by-broker-de.html)[[134]](https://ijsrcseit.com/paper/CSEIT23906203.pdf)[[146]](https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P14112024.pdf).  
5. **Explainability must be operational, not cosmetic.** If a model cannot support post-trade attribution and oversight, its deployment burden rises sharply [[150]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2022/october/artificial-intelligence)[[156]](https://www.iif.com/portals/0/Files/content/Innovation/2024%20IIF-EY%20Survey%20Report%20on%20AI_ML%20Use%20in%20Financial%20Services_Public%2001.08.25.pdf).

## Limitations of Current Evidence and Open Research Problems

### Limitations of current evidence

- **DL evidence is large but heterogeneous.** Systematic reviews show rapid growth, but the literature spans different assets, objectives, data inputs, and benchmark standards [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591).  
- **Many direct comparisons are not apples-to-apples.** Objectives, cost models, leverage assumptions, and information sets often differ [[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591).  
- **Some useful direct comparisons come from lower-tier evidence.** For example, thesis-based BL vs MVO studies provide concrete implementation detail but carry less weight than broad peer-reviewed or institutional studies [[102]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:758241/FULLTEXT01).  
- **Replicability remains limited.** Many hybrid and end-to-end approaches are recent, and several are preprint-stage or not widely reproduced [[121]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.11273)[[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1)[[123]](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.01918v3).  
- **Regime dependence is under-modeled.** Average backtest superiority often hides failure in structural breaks [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[145]](https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/financial-stability-publications/fsr/special/html/ecb.fsrart202405_02~58c3ce5246.en.html).

### Open research problems

1. **Causality vs correlation mining.** Most DL systems still learn predictive regularities rather than economic causal structure [[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf).  
2. **Confidence calibration in BL.** Practical selection of \( \tau \) and \( \Omega \) remains under-specified [[58]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1800205/FULLTEXT01.pdf)[[75]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:372812/FULLTEXT01.pdf)[[76]](https://sites.duke.edu/djepapers/files/2016/10/danielroeder-dje.original.pdf).  
3. **Interpretable deep portfolio models.** Explainable AI tools remain partial, especially for RL and end-to-end allocators [[52]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.14486v1)[[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf)[[151]](https://wrap.warwick.ac.uk/id/eprint/176737/).  
4. **Tail-event robustness.** Variance-centric systems remain weak under crisis dependence and jump risk [[71]](https://d-nb.info/1078505004/34)[[98]](https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/portalfiles/portal/94044423/Dynamic_Black_Litterman.pdf)[[132]](https://arxiv.org/html/2604.14206v1).  
5. **Online adaptation without overfitting.** Regime detection and retraining policies are still unstable research areas [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[86]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2025-02/ESMA50-43599798-9923_TRV_Article_Artificial_intelligence_in_EU_investment_funds.pdf)[[94]](https://communities.springernature.com/posts/building-a-machine-learning-framework-for-dynamic-risk-based-asset-allocation).  
6. **Standardized benchmarking.** The field still lacks broadly accepted protocols that align objective functions, costs, and information sets across model families [[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[112]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/8.4-backtesting-market-data.html).

## Implications for Practitioners, FinTech Platforms, and Investment Governance

### Practical decision table

| Decision context | Best fit | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Low data richness, high governance needs** | Constrained MVO | Most transparent and operationally mature [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[150]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2022/october/artificial-intelligence) |
| **Institutional allocation with benchmark and explicit views** | BL | Best native framework for disciplined tilts away from equilibrium [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[103]](https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/eurjfi/v23y2017i1p1-30.html) |
| **Data-rich tactical allocation** | DL + classical optimizer | DL adds signal extraction; optimizer preserves implementation control [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf)[[121]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.11273) |
| **Need to combine discretionary and systematic views** | BL or ML→BL hybrid | Views and confidence remain explicit [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[53]](https://www.engineeringletters.com/issues_v29/issue_2/EL_29_2_40.pdf)[[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1) |
| **Maximum explainability required** | MVO, then BL | Easier committee and regulatory communication than standalone DL [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf) |
| **Need regime adaptation under high volatility** | Hybrid DL + BL + constrained optimizer | Combines flexibility with shrinkage and discipline [[94]](https://communities.springernature.com/posts/building-a-machine-learning-framework-for-dynamic-risk-based-asset-allocation)[[121]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.11273)[[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1) |
| **Retail or regulated advisory platform** | BL or constrained MVO with light ML overlays | Lower governance burden than black-box RL [[91]](https://www.aima.org/article/a-practical-guide-for-advisers-considering-the-use-of-ai.html)[[150]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2022/october/artificial-intelligence)[[156]](https://www.iif.com/portals/0/Files/content/Innovation/2024%20IIF-EY%20Survey%20Report%20on%20AI_ML%20Use%20in%20Financial%20Services_Public%2001.08.25.pdf) |

### Implications by firm type

ESMA’s adoption data imply a practical split by organizational scale [[88]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2026-02/ESMA50-481369926-30599_TRV_Risk_Analysis_AI_adoption_and_trends_in_securities_markets.pdf).

- **Large firms** are much more able to deploy hybrid stacks because they have data engineering, model validation, and governance resources; this is consistent with the finding that **96%** of large firms were already using AI or planning to do so [[88]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2026-02/ESMA50-481369926-30599_TRV_Risk_Analysis_AI_adoption_and_trends_in_securities_markets.pdf).
- **Micro and smaller firms** are more likely to remain with BL, constrained MVO, or vendor-provided AI overlays because infrastructure and oversight costs are high; this fits the finding that **65%** of micro firms had no active or planned AI use cases [[88]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2026-02/ESMA50-481369926-30599_TRV_Risk_Analysis_AI_adoption_and_trends_in_securities_markets.pdf).

This is not just a budget issue. It is a governance issue: the more complex the model stack, the more demanding the requirements for data lineage, monitoring, override procedures, and regulatory defensibility [[145]](https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/financial-stability-publications/fsr/special/html/ecb.fsrart202405_02~58c3ce5246.en.html)[[146]](https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P14112024.pdf)[[150]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2022/october/artificial-intelligence)[[156]](https://www.iif.com/portals/0/Files/content/Innovation/2024%20IIF-EY%20Survey%20Report%20on%20AI_ML%20Use%20in%20Financial%20Services_Public%2001.08.25.pdf).

### Implications for platform design

A credible FinTech allocation platform should therefore avoid claiming that AI replaces portfolio theory. A more defensible architecture is:

- **AI for research, feature extraction, and signal generation**  
- **Bayesian regularization for converting uncertain signals into investment views**  
- **explicit optimization and risk controls for final portfolio construction**  
- **continuous monitoring, documentation, and human oversight** [[86]](https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2025-02/ESMA50-43599798-9923_TRV_Article_Artificial_intelligence_in_EU_investment_funds.pdf)[[87]](https://www.imf.org/-/media/files/publications/gfsr/2024/october/english/ch3.pdf)[[89]](https://www.dechert.com/knowledge/onpoint/2023/9/sec-proposes-new-regulatory-framework-for-use-of-ai-by-broker-de.html)[[91]](https://www.aima.org/article/a-practical-guide-for-advisers-considering-the-use-of-ai.html)

That design also matches the current regulatory climate more closely than fully autonomous black-box allocation.

## Evidence Matrix

| Major claim | Representative source(s) | Context / dataset | Main finding | Evidence quality note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| MVO is highly sensitive to estimation error | [[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[67]](https://users.nber.org/~confer/2006/si2006/ap/uppal.pdf)[[1]](https://www.andrew.cmu.edu/user/gc0v/webpub/estimationerror.pdf) | Classical portfolio settings | Small errors in \( \mu \) or \( \Sigma \) can create extreme unstable weights | Strong mechanism support; widely replicated |

## Conclusion

The comparison is clearest once the portfolio process is decomposed into **return formation, risk modeling, and allocation**.

- **Mean-Variance Optimization** is the most transparent and operationally mature **allocation engine**. It is strongest when governance, explicit constraints, and implementation control matter most. It is weakest when expected returns are noisy, covariance estimates are unstable, and return distributions are non-normal [[49]](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/papers/pdf/markowitz.pdf)[[5]](https://docs.mosek.com/portfolio-cookbook/estimationerror.html)[[64]](https://stacks.stanford.edu/file/druid:zm187qb0188/2009-08.pdf)[[67]](https://users.nber.org/~confer/2006/si2006/ap/uppal.pdf)[[71]](https://d-nb.info/1078505004/34).
- **Black-Litterman** is the strongest standalone framework for **regularizing expected returns**. It improves on naïve MVO by anchoring allocations to equilibrium and allowing explicit, confidence-weighted tilts. Its main weakness is not mathematics but judgment: results depend on the quality of views and calibration choices [[11]](https://people.duke.edu/~charvey/Teaching/BA453_2006/Idzorek_onBL.pdf)[[50]](https://pyportfolioopt.readthedocs.io/en/stable/BlackLitterman.html)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf)[[58]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1800205/FULLTEXT01.pdf)[[75]](https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:372812/FULLTEXT01.pdf).
- **Deep learning** is the most powerful family for **nonlinear prediction and representation learning**. It is strongest when data are rich, nonlinear, and regime-sensitive. It is weakest when explainability, stability, and cost-aware deployment are under-specified [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1059056025008822)[[32]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.01591)[[137]](https://www.oxford-man.ox.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Deep-Learning-for-Portfolio-Optimisation.pdf)[[138]](https://eprints.soton.ac.uk/495610/1/1-s2.0-S1044028324000887-main.pdf)[[140]](http://ww.sentic.net/financial-explainable-ai-review.pdf)[[146]](https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P14112024.pdf).

**Final judgment on the core question:**  
A **general-purpose hybrid framework is feasible and, under current evidence, the most realistic route to combining the strengths of all three families**. The most defensible design is:
1. **DL for signal extraction and regime awareness**,  
2. **BL/Bayesian updating for confidence-aware return regularization**, and  
3. **constrained classical optimization for final portfolio construction and governance** [[53]](https://www.engineeringletters.com/issues_v29/issue_2/EL_29_2_40.pdf)[[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.02185v1)[[121]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.11273)[[123]](https://arxiv.org/html/2507.01918v3)[[100]](https://cims.nyu.edu/~ritter/kolm2021black.pdf).

But that conclusion is conditional. A hybrid is superior only if it is paired with:
- **rigorous walk-forward validation**,  
- **explicit transaction-cost and turnover control**,  
- **uncertainty calibration**,  
- **drift monitoring**, and  
- **human oversight and documentation** [[31]](https://icaps23.icaps-conference.org/papers/finplan/FinPlan23_paper_4.pdf)[[112]](https://portfoliooptimizationbook.com/book/8.4-backtesting-market-data.html)[[89]](https://www.dechert.com/knowledge/onpoint/2023/9/sec-proposes-new-regulatory-framework-for-use-of-ai-by-broker-de.html)[[145]](https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/financial-stability-publications/fsr/special/html/ecb.fsrart202405_02~58c3ce5246.en.html)[[156]](https://www.iif.com/portals/0/Files/content/Innovation/2024%20IIF-EY%20Survey%20Report%20on%20AI_ML%20Use%20in%20Financial%20Services_Public%2001.08.25.pdf).

So the most accurate answer is not “replace classical portfolio theory with deep learning.” It is:

**Use deep learning where nonlinear forecasting and regime detection matter; use Black-Litterman where uncertain views need disciplined shrinkage; and use classical optimization where portfolio decisions must remain stable, implementable, and governable.**
